Research shows debugging consumes up to 75% of developer time and AI-generated code is making it worse, here’s what the data says and how to fix the process.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Ask most developers where their time goes, and “writing code” is the honest first answer. The data says otherwise. Depending on the study, developers spend somewhere between 35% and 75% of their working time on debugging rather than writing new code, and fixing an existing bug has been measured at taking roughly 30 times longer than writing the original line that caused it.
That gap has always existed. What’s new is that AI-generated code is adding to it rather than shrinking it, according to recent developer survey data. This piece looks at what the actual numbers say about where debugging time goes, why AI hasn’t fixed the problem yet, and what a more structured approach to debugging looks like in practice.

What the Data Actually Says
The scale of the debugging problem isn’t a matter of opinion. Industry analysis compiled from multiple engineering studies puts the average developer at creating roughly 70 bugs per 1,000 lines of code, with about 15 of those per 1,000 lines making it all the way to production. The same analysis estimates that fixing a bug takes around 30 times longer than writing the original line, and that debugging consumes as much as 75% of a developer’s working year, translating to roughly 1,500 hours annually spent finding and fixing defects rather than building new functionality.
Academic research tells a consistent story from a different angle. A 2002 study on embedded and safety-critical software found engineers spending 70–80% of their time on testing and debugging combined. More recent exploratory research tracking developers directly during debugging sessions found that a large share of that time, 61 to 74% depending on the task — goes not to the actual defect-solving reasoning, but to navigating the development environment itself: opening files, running programs to observe output, and consulting external resources to understand what’s actually happening.
That detail matters. It means a meaningful share of debugging time isn’t spent thinking about the bug, it’s spent on process overhead around finding and reproducing it. That’s exactly the kind of cost a more structured, repeatable process can reduce, independent of how skilled any individual developer is.

Why AI Hasn’t Solved This Yet
The intuitive expectation was that AI-generated code would shrink debugging time by writing more correct code up front. The data on this so far tells a different story.
Stack Overflow’s 2025 Developer Survey found that 66% of developers describe AI-generated code as “almost right, but not quite”, a category of output that’s specifically harder to debug than code that’s obviously wrong, because it looks plausible enough to pass an initial read. A separate industry survey found that 45.2% of respondents said debugging AI-generated code takes more time than debugging code a human wrote, precisely because there’s no shared mental model of intent to check the output against.
The pattern that emerges is that AI assistance shifted where the cost sits, rather than removing it. Time saved on typing the first draft is being spent instead on reading, verifying, and correcting output that looks more confident than it is, which is a worse trade than it sounds, because confident-looking wrong code is measurably harder for a human reviewer to catch than code that reads as obviously incomplete.

What a More Structured Debugging Process Actually Changes
Given that a large share of debugging time goes to process overhead rather than the core reasoning task, the highest-leverage fix isn’t “get better at debugging” as a vague skill, it’s making the process itself more repeatable, so less time gets spent re-deriving the same steps for every new bug.
A few principles show up consistently in the research on what separates efficient debugging from inefficient debugging:
Reproduction before investigation. Time spent confirming a bug happens reliably, and under what specific conditions, prevents the far more expensive mistake of “fixing” something that was never actually broken the way it first appeared.
Isolating the smallest failing case before reading broadly. The environment-navigation overhead identified in the research above, opening files, running programs, checking output — gets sharply reduced once the search space is narrowed to a minimal reproduction, rather than being repeated across an entire codebase.
Treating AI-suggested fixes as hypotheses, not answers. Given that “almost right” is the most common AI failure mode reported by developers, a fix suggested by an assistant needs the same verification step as a fix a junior developer proposes, checked against the actual reproduction case, not accepted because it looks plausible.
Logging what was tried, not just what worked. A meaningful share of debugging time is process overhead specifically because context gets rebuilt from scratch for every new session on a hard bug. A simple record of ruled-out hypotheses prevents re-investigating the same dead end twice.

Practical Checklist

• Confirm the bug reproduces reliably before starting to investigate a cause

• Narrow to the smallest failing case before reading broadly across the codebase

• Treat AI-suggested fixes as hypotheses to verify against the reproduction case, not final answers

• Keep a running log of ruled-out causes during long debugging sessions

• Track how much of your debugging time is process overhead versus actual defect reasoning

FAQ
Is 75% of developer time really spent debugging? Estimates vary by study and codebase maturity, ranging from roughly 35% to 75% the honest range is wide, but every credible source puts debugging as a larger share of developer time than writing new code.
Why does fixing a bug take so much longer than writing the original code? Writing code involves producing one plausible solution; debugging involves ruling out many implausible ones first, since the cause usually isn’t obvious from where the failure appears.
Does AI-assisted coding reduce total debugging time? Current survey data suggests not yet — a large share of developers report debugging AI-generated code takes as long or longer than debugging human-written code, largely because of the “almost right” failure pattern.
What’s the single highest-leverage change to reduce debugging time? Based on the research on where debugging time actually goes, narrowing to a minimal reproducible case before broad investigation cuts the environment-navigation overhead that consumes a majority of many debugging sessions.
Should AI-suggested fixes be trusted more or less than a colleague’s suggestion? Treat them the same way — verify against the actual reproduction case rather than accepting a fix because it reads as confident or complete.

Conclusion
The data is consistent across two decades of research: debugging, not writing, is where most developer time actually goes, and a meaningful share of that time is process overhead rather than genuine problem-solving. AI-generated code hasn’t fixed this yet — in some measured cases, it’s added a new debugging tax on top of the old one, because “almost right” output is harder to catch than obviously wrong output.
Reducing that cost consistently comes down to structure: reliable reproduction, narrow isolation, treating suggested fixes as hypotheses, and tracking what’s already been ruled out. For anyone who wants that structure already laid out as a repeatable system rather than built from scratch under deadline pressure, the AI Debugging System playbook documents exactly this kind of process, step by step.

Sources
Coralogix, “This Is What Your Developers Are Doing 75% of the Time, and This Is the Cost You Pay” — https://coralogix.com/blog/this-is-what-your-developers-are-doing-75-of-the-time-and-this-is-the-cost-you-pay/
Arafa, Solomon, Navabpour, Fischmeister, “Debugging Behaviour of Embedded-Software Developers: An Exploratory Study” — https://arxiv.org/pdf/1704.03397
Alaboudi, LaToza, “An Exploratory Study of Debugging Episodes” — https://arxiv.org/pdf/2105.02162
Stack Overflow, 2025 Developer Survey: AI section — https://survey.stackoverflow.co/2025/ai/
Syncause, “Industry Survey: Faster Coding, Slower Debugging” — https://syn-cause.com/blog/debug-time-increased
